USS Lincoln Hit: Hypersonic Missile Strikes Carrier

USS Lincoln Hit: Hypersonic Missile Strikes Carrier

In an unprecedented escalation that has sent shockwaves through global defense ministries, investigations have confirmed that the U.S. Navy aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72) has been struck by an Iranian precision-guided hypersonic missile. The attack, which involved a barrage of four medium-range ballistic and hypersonic missiles, succeeded in penetrating the carrier strike group's (CSG) multi-layered defensive screen.

This strike, combined with the successful interdiction of the group's ammunition and fuel replenishment vessels, has severely degraded the USS Abraham Lincoln's operational velocity and flight deck capabilities, placing U.S. naval forces in a defensive posture.

Penetrating the Carrier's Defense Screen

A direct attack on an active U.S. aircraft carrier—the most heavily protected mobile military installation on Earth—was previously considered highly improbable by Western planners. According to a statement released by the Armed Forces of the Islamic Republic of Iran and carried by the Tasnim News Agency, the carrier was targeted while operating in the Indian Ocean.

Military reports indicate the details of the engagement:

  • Hypersonic Penetration: While U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) initially claimed that all incoming threats—including loitering munitions and anti-ship ballistic missiles—were successfully intercepted, subsequent analysis confirmed that at least one Fattah-1 hypersonic glide vehicle bypassed the Aegis combat systems.
  • The Maneuvering Threat: The missile, traveling at speeds exceeding Mach 13 with a maneuvering reentry vehicle (MaRV), adjusted its flight path during the terminal phase to target the moving carrier, scoring a direct hit.
  • Operational Degradation: The impact has caused structural damage to the flight deck and propulsion systems, restricting the speed at which the carrier can maneuver and limiting its ability to launch and recover aircraft.

This kinetic strike follows the successful logistics siege on the USS Abraham Lincoln Carrier Strike Group, which cut off the carrier's ammunition supply at Jebel Ali and fuel replenishment in the Indian Ocean.

The USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72) operating in the Indian Ocean, where it was recently struck by an Iranian hypersonic missile.

Force Protection and Regional Evacuations

The direct threat to U.S. assets has forced CENTCOM to implement emergency force protection measures across the region. With the U.S. Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain damaged and the Kuwait naval base heavily struck, U.S. planners are prioritizing the safety of their personnel:

  1. Confinement in Bahrain and UAE: U.S. service members stationed in Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates have been restricted to secure military quarters and hotels, with all off-base liberties suspended.
  2. Amman Embassy Restrictions: American personnel in Jordan have been banned from entering the U.S. Embassy compound in Amman due to intelligence indicating potential retaliatory rocket or drone strikes on diplomatic facilities.
  3. Logistical Retreat: The destruction of base infrastructure has forced a strategic retreat of non-essential personnel, leaving regional bases operating with skeleton crews.

The Air War and the Search for a Ceasefire

Despite the damage to U.S. assets, the air war continues with high intensity. Israeli Air Force (IAF) pilots are reportedly operating under emergency deployment schedules, with individual aircraft carrying out up to three combat sorties daily. The Israeli campaign has focused on striking Basij headquarters and government installations inside Tehran, aiming to trigger domestic instability as part of Prime Minister Netanyahu's broader regime collapse scenario.

In response, Iranian forces have launched counter-strikes targeting locations near Beit Shemesh in central Israel. The mutual bombardment of the two nations' depths has complicated diplomatic efforts.

While the Trump administration seeks a rapid de-escalation to protect its regional bases, the damage to the USS Abraham Lincoln and the blindness of the early warning radar network in Qatar have degraded the U.S. bargaining position, leaving regional security in a highly volatile state.

item.title: Bond Yields Rise: Why the Fed Is Making a Mistake
item.author: khalid
item.description: Bond yields are rising, but TIPS and consumer surveys show it's not inflation. The Fed is about to repeat a historic rate-hiking policy mistake.
item.yandex:full-text: Bond Yields Rise: Why the Fed Is Making a Policy Mistake Bond yields rose sharply on Friday, but why? The mainstream narrative is that strong economic growth and sticky inflation are forcing the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates. But that is only half true. The Fed might indeed be gearing up to hike rates, but the underlying reason is not a booming economy or runaway inflation. Both the financial markets and American consumers agree on this. The Inflation Myth vs. The Policy Mistake While nominal interest rates have drifted higher, inflation expectations in the Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) market have dropped to their lowest levels since the start of the conflict. The market is not pricing in a structural return of inflation. Instead, it is signaling that the Federal Reserve is preparing to make a classic policy error: panicking over oil prices and hiking rates directly into a weakening economy. American consumers completely share this outlook. The latest Survey of Consumer Expectations from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York shows no sign of unanchored inflation psychology. Short-term inflation expectations are moving down, longer-term expectations remain anchored, credit conditions are deteriorating, and households are increasingly worried about their jobs. Mainstream commentators are paying attention to lagging jobs data, but the underlying forward-looking indicators tell a very different story. Central bankers are preparing to react to short-term energy shocks, threatening to repeat the same demand-destruction policy errors of the past. What is the TIPS Market Actually Telling Us? You rarely hear the mainstream media discuss the Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) market, even though it is the largest market explicitly dedicated to pricing inflation expectations. TIPS principal adjusts with the Consumer Price Index (CPI). The spread between a nominal Treasury yield and the real yield on a TIPS of the same maturity is the "break-even inflation rate." If the bond market were genuinely concerned about sustained, runaway inflation, we would see break-evens surge. Instead, the opposite is happening: Underwhelming Levels : The 5-year break-even previously ticked up toward 270 basis points. However, this is far below the 325–350 basis points seen during the broader supply shock of 2022. Recent Decline : Even as oil and gasoline prices remain elevated due to the ongoing conflict and tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, the 5-year break-even rate recently fell back to around 248 basis points. Yield Curve Inversion : Shorter-term break-evens remain higher than longer-term ones. This curve inversion demonstrates that the market views the current situation as a temporary energy-led CPI impulse, not embedded inflation. Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals , lets you do. Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it. If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you. Consumer Psychology Refuses to Unanchor The Federal Reserve's own consumer surveys contradict the hawkish rhetoric of its governors. The New York Fed's May Survey of Consumer Expectations shows that: Near-Term Inflation Expectations : Median one-year inflation expectations decreased by a tenth of a percentage point to 3.5% . Medium-Term Expectations : Three-year and five-year expectations remained unchanged at 3.0% , right around their long-term historical averages. Credit Strains : Perceptions of credit availability deteriorated, with households reporting that credit is becoming harder to access. Labor Market Worry : The perceived probability of finding a job if one's current job is lost fell by 2.3 percentage points to 43.7% —the lowest level since December 2025. Meanwhile, the probability of losing a job rose to 15.1% . Americans are not worried about a wage-price spiral. They are worried about keeping their jobs and accessing credit. The Two-Year Yield: Hedging Against Fed Panic If inflation is not the driver, why did the nominal 2-year Treasury yield rise so sharply? The 2-year Treasury is highly sensitive to short-term Federal Reserve policy. Traders are selling the 2-year not because they expect inflation to spiral, but to hedge against Fed panic. They know from history that central bankers often react to short-term, supply-driven headline CPI spikes with rate hikes. Meanwhile, other parts of the money market are pricing in a very different reality. Treasury bill yields and term SOFR futures are hedging for economic weakness, pricing in eventual rate cuts as credit conditions tighten. The market is simultaneously pricing two risks: Near-Term : The Fed panics over oil prices and hikes rates (hedged by selling the 2-year Treasury). Medium-Term : The rate hikes accelerate demand destruction, forcing the Fed to pivot and cut rates (hedged in the SOFR futures curve). Conclusion: Another Trichet Moment This macro setup strongly resembles 2008 and 2011, when Jean-Claude Trichet famously hiked rates at the European Central Bank. Trichet panicked over oil-driven inflation indicators right as the global economy was fracturing. High oil prices are not a sign of economic strength; they act as a tax on the consumer. When gas prices spike, households immediately cut discretionary spending. This leads to demand destruction, which resolves the price pressures on its own. If policy makers mistake a supply-side energy shock for broad-based inflation, they risk hiking rates directly into a recessionary trap. Both the bond market and consumers have read the indicators—it's time for the Fed to do the same. This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on credit markets, central bank policy, and systemic consumer cycles. item.turbo:content:
Bond Yields Rise: Why the Fed Is Making a Mistake

Bond Yields Rise: Why the Fed Is Making a Policy Mistake

Bond yields rose sharply on Friday, but why? The mainstream narrative is that strong economic growth and sticky inflation are forcing the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates.

But that is only half true. The Fed might indeed be gearing up to hike rates, but the underlying reason is not a booming economy or runaway inflation. Both the financial markets and American consumers agree on this.

The Inflation Myth vs. The Policy Mistake

While nominal interest rates have drifted higher, inflation expectations in the Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) market have dropped to their lowest levels since the start of the conflict. The market is not pricing in a structural return of inflation. Instead, it is signaling that the Federal Reserve is preparing to make a classic policy error: panicking over oil prices and hiking rates directly into a weakening economy.

American consumers completely share this outlook. The latest Survey of Consumer Expectations from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York shows no sign of unanchored inflation psychology. Short-term inflation expectations are moving down, longer-term expectations remain anchored, credit conditions are deteriorating, and households are increasingly worried about their jobs.

Mainstream commentators are paying attention to lagging jobs data, but the underlying forward-looking indicators tell a very different story. Central bankers are preparing to react to short-term energy shocks, threatening to repeat the same demand-destruction policy errors of the past.

What is the TIPS Market Actually Telling Us?

You rarely hear the mainstream media discuss the Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) market, even though it is the largest market explicitly dedicated to pricing inflation expectations.

TIPS principal adjusts with the Consumer Price Index (CPI). The spread between a nominal Treasury yield and the real yield on a TIPS of the same maturity is the "break-even inflation rate." If the bond market were genuinely concerned about sustained, runaway inflation, we would see break-evens surge.

Instead, the opposite is happening:

  • Underwhelming Levels: The 5-year break-even previously ticked up toward 270 basis points. However, this is far below the 325–350 basis points seen during the broader supply shock of 2022.
  • Recent Decline: Even as oil and gasoline prices remain elevated due to the ongoing conflict and tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, the 5-year break-even rate recently fell back to around 248 basis points.
  • Yield Curve Inversion: Shorter-term break-evens remain higher than longer-term ones. This curve inversion demonstrates that the market views the current situation as a temporary energy-led CPI impulse, not embedded inflation.

Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals

What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals, lets you do.

Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it.

If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you.

Consumer Psychology Refuses to Unanchor

The Federal Reserve's own consumer surveys contradict the hawkish rhetoric of its governors. The New York Fed's May Survey of Consumer Expectations shows that:

  • Near-Term Inflation Expectations: Median one-year inflation expectations decreased by a tenth of a percentage point to 3.5%.
  • Medium-Term Expectations: Three-year and five-year expectations remained unchanged at 3.0%, right around their long-term historical averages.
  • Credit Strains: Perceptions of credit availability deteriorated, with households reporting that credit is becoming harder to access.
  • Labor Market Worry: The perceived probability of finding a job if one's current job is lost fell by 2.3 percentage points to 43.7%—the lowest level since December 2025. Meanwhile, the probability of losing a job rose to 15.1%.

Americans are not worried about a wage-price spiral. They are worried about keeping their jobs and accessing credit.

The Two-Year Yield: Hedging Against Fed Panic

If inflation is not the driver, why did the nominal 2-year Treasury yield rise so sharply?

The 2-year Treasury is highly sensitive to short-term Federal Reserve policy. Traders are selling the 2-year not because they expect inflation to spiral, but to hedge against Fed panic. They know from history that central bankers often react to short-term, supply-driven headline CPI spikes with rate hikes.

Meanwhile, other parts of the money market are pricing in a very different reality. Treasury bill yields and term SOFR futures are hedging for economic weakness, pricing in eventual rate cuts as credit conditions tighten.

The market is simultaneously pricing two risks:

  1. Near-Term: The Fed panics over oil prices and hikes rates (hedged by selling the 2-year Treasury).
  2. Medium-Term: The rate hikes accelerate demand destruction, forcing the Fed to pivot and cut rates (hedged in the SOFR futures curve).

Conclusion: Another Trichet Moment

This macro setup strongly resembles 2008 and 2011, when Jean-Claude Trichet famously hiked rates at the European Central Bank. Trichet panicked over oil-driven inflation indicators right as the global economy was fracturing.

High oil prices are not a sign of economic strength; they act as a tax on the consumer. When gas prices spike, households immediately cut discretionary spending. This leads to demand destruction, which resolves the price pressures on its own.

If policy makers mistake a supply-side energy shock for broad-based inflation, they risk hiking rates directly into a recessionary trap. Both the bond market and consumers have read the indicators—it's time for the Fed to do the same.

This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on credit markets, central bank policy, and systemic consumer cycles.

item.title: The Credit Cycle: Where Are We in the Downswing?
item.author: khalid
item.description: Private credit is under pressure as redemptions rise. Understanding the four stages of the credit cycle and why we have entered a systemic downswing.
item.yandex:full-text: The Credit Cycle: Where Are We in the Downswing? What is a credit cycle and where are we in it? These are the questions increasingly coming up as stress ripples across the credit markets. Most of the noise is concentrated in private credit, where the expansion was loudest. But to understand the true macroeconomic picture, you have to think much more broadly. The Self-Reinforcing Nature of Lending Booms A credit cycle is simply the expansion and contraction of lending. At its heart, however, it is a cycle of trust. Lenders, borrowers, and investors act in herds, reinforcing trends until reality forces a reversal. During the upswing, credit becomes easier a little at a time: Lenders compete for deals by loosening loan covenants. Borrowers secure better terms and higher leverage. Investors accept lower yields for higher credit risk. Collateral values rise, and defaults remain low. Because defaults stay low during the boom, everyone concludes that lending standards were appropriate. Hyman Minsky described this dynamic decades ago: stability breeds instability . The longer conditions look calm, the more risk participants take because calm conditions make risk look smaller than it actually is. Bad lending doesn't look bad when the loan is made. It looks bad later, when the assumptions behind it—like stable interest rates and constant earnings growth—are proven false. The Four Stages of the Credit Cycle Every credit cycle, regardless of the historical era, moves through four distinct phases: Stage 1: Repair After a major bust, lenders are cautious and investors nurse their losses. Credit is available only to the strongest borrowers. basic economic survival and scar tissue dictate the market. Stage 2: Expansion As the economy improves, defaults fall. Yield-starved investors return, and lenders find that cautious post-bust loans are performing well. Credit availability improves, and spreads begin to tighten. Stage 3: Excess Too much capital begins chasing too few quality loans. Competition forces lenders to compromise on covenants. Debt structures become complex—showing up as CLOs, payment-in-kind (PIK) interest, and optimistic model-based valuations. Stage 4: The Turn Growth slows, a specific sector faces stress, or the refinancing window begins to close. The turn starts quietly. At first, problems are dismissed as isolated incidents. But soon, the same loose assumptions are exposed across the entire system, and the cycle flips into a downswing. Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals , lets you do. Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it. If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you. Why the Fed Doesn't Control the Cycle A common textbook misconception is that the Federal Reserve controls the credit cycle via interest rate policy. History suggests otherwise. For instance, the Fed raised rates steadily from 2004 to 2006. Yet, the worst subprime mortgage excesses occurred during that exact period. The credit machine was not driven by the Fed's overnight rate; it was driven by global demand for yield, securitization structures, and rising collateral values. Similarly, the aggressive rate hikes of 2022 and 2023 did not immediately end the recent private credit boom. Instead, the cycle kept expanding as funds delayed defaults by modifying terms or allowing borrowers to pay interest with more debt (PIK). The turn only materialized when real economic cracks appeared in the labor market, exposing the structural slowdown we explored in our analysis of home sales plunging to 2010 levels and rising bond yields . The Shadow Banking Center of Gravity Every credit cycle has a center of gravity. In 2008, it was subprime mortgages held by commercial banks. Today, it is private credit issued by non-bank financial institutions (shadow banks). Private credit ballooned post-2008 by offering a way for private equity sponsors to secure financing without public market disclosure. But the structural weaknesses of this system are now surfacing: Illiquidity : The underlying loans cannot be easily sold. Model-Based Valuations : Marks are based on internal models, not market transactions. Redemption Pressures : Investors are asking for their cash back, forcing major funds (including giants like Blackstone) to restrict withdrawals. BDC Discounts : Publicly traded Business Development Companies (BDCs) are trading below net asset value (NAV), signaling that the market does not trust the official marks. Conclusion: Navigating the Downswing We have transitioned past the peak of the cycle. We are currently in the downswing, where the primary concern has shifted from "how do I get more yield" to "how do I get my cash back" . As refinancing costs remain high and corporate earnings feel the pinch of demand destruction, the private credit downswing will continue to pull on the rest of the financial system. For macro observers, tracking these subtle shifts in lending standards, credit availability, and investor redemptions is crucial to understanding what lies ahead. This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on shadow banking plumbing, credit cycles, and corporate debt markets. Monitor global macroeconomic indicators, options volume, and liquidity regimes in real-time with Dashboard Options .
item.turbo:content:
The Credit Cycle: Where Are We in the Downswing?

The Credit Cycle: Where Are We in the Downswing?

What is a credit cycle and where are we in it? These are the questions increasingly coming up as stress ripples across the credit markets.

Most of the noise is concentrated in private credit, where the expansion was loudest. But to understand the true macroeconomic picture, you have to think much more broadly.

The Self-Reinforcing Nature of Lending Booms

A credit cycle is simply the expansion and contraction of lending. At its heart, however, it is a cycle of trust. Lenders, borrowers, and investors act in herds, reinforcing trends until reality forces a reversal.

During the upswing, credit becomes easier a little at a time:

  • Lenders compete for deals by loosening loan covenants.
  • Borrowers secure better terms and higher leverage.
  • Investors accept lower yields for higher credit risk.
  • Collateral values rise, and defaults remain low.

Because defaults stay low during the boom, everyone concludes that lending standards were appropriate. Hyman Minsky described this dynamic decades ago: stability breeds instability. The longer conditions look calm, the more risk participants take because calm conditions make risk look smaller than it actually is.

Bad lending doesn't look bad when the loan is made. It looks bad later, when the assumptions behind it—like stable interest rates and constant earnings growth—are proven false.

The Four Stages of the Credit Cycle

Every credit cycle, regardless of the historical era, moves through four distinct phases:

Stage 1: Repair

After a major bust, lenders are cautious and investors nurse their losses. Credit is available only to the strongest borrowers. basic economic survival and scar tissue dictate the market.

Stage 2: Expansion

As the economy improves, defaults fall. Yield-starved investors return, and lenders find that cautious post-bust loans are performing well. Credit availability improves, and spreads begin to tighten.

Stage 3: Excess

Too much capital begins chasing too few quality loans. Competition forces lenders to compromise on covenants. Debt structures become complex—showing up as CLOs, payment-in-kind (PIK) interest, and optimistic model-based valuations.

Stage 4: The Turn

Growth slows, a specific sector faces stress, or the refinancing window begins to close. The turn starts quietly. At first, problems are dismissed as isolated incidents. But soon, the same loose assumptions are exposed across the entire system, and the cycle flips into a downswing.

Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals

What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals, lets you do.

Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it.

If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you.

Why the Fed Doesn't Control the Cycle

A common textbook misconception is that the Federal Reserve controls the credit cycle via interest rate policy. History suggests otherwise.

For instance, the Fed raised rates steadily from 2004 to 2006. Yet, the worst subprime mortgage excesses occurred during that exact period. The credit machine was not driven by the Fed's overnight rate; it was driven by global demand for yield, securitization structures, and rising collateral values.

Similarly, the aggressive rate hikes of 2022 and 2023 did not immediately end the recent private credit boom. Instead, the cycle kept expanding as funds delayed defaults by modifying terms or allowing borrowers to pay interest with more debt (PIK).

The turn only materialized when real economic cracks appeared in the labor market, exposing the structural slowdown we explored in our analysis of home sales plunging to 2010 levels and rising bond yields.

The Shadow Banking Center of Gravity

Every credit cycle has a center of gravity. In 2008, it was subprime mortgages held by commercial banks. Today, it is private credit issued by non-bank financial institutions (shadow banks).

Private credit ballooned post-2008 by offering a way for private equity sponsors to secure financing without public market disclosure. But the structural weaknesses of this system are now surfacing:

  1. Illiquidity: The underlying loans cannot be easily sold.
  2. Model-Based Valuations: Marks are based on internal models, not market transactions.
  3. Redemption Pressures: Investors are asking for their cash back, forcing major funds (including giants like Blackstone) to restrict withdrawals.
  4. BDC Discounts: Publicly traded Business Development Companies (BDCs) are trading below net asset value (NAV), signaling that the market does not trust the official marks.

Conclusion: Navigating the Downswing

We have transitioned past the peak of the cycle. We are currently in the downswing, where the primary concern has shifted from "how do I get more yield" to "how do I get my cash back".

As refinancing costs remain high and corporate earnings feel the pinch of demand destruction, the private credit downswing will continue to pull on the rest of the financial system. For macro observers, tracking these subtle shifts in lending standards, credit availability, and investor redemptions is crucial to understanding what lies ahead.

This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on shadow banking plumbing, credit cycles, and corporate debt markets.

item.title: ECB Policy Mistake: Why European Banks Buy Safety
item.author: khalid
item.description: European banks are loading up on government bonds. Understanding the Euribor futures frown and why the market rejects the ECB's inflation rhetoric.
item.yandex:full-text: ECB Policy Mistake: Why European Banks Buy Safety European banks are doing something that looks, at first glance, completely irrational. While mainstream commentators focus on central bank hawkishness, the European Central Bank (ECB) recently raised its policy rates and officials continue to threaten further hikes, fixated on inflation risks driven by the energy shock. Yet, the banks themselves are preparing for a very different reality. The Inflation Illusion vs. Real Credit Risk Normally, if you expect interest rates to go higher, you avoid government bonds to protect yourself from duration risk and mark-to-market losses. But European depository institutions are aggressively buying government debt, signaling that they do not believe the ECB's inflation narrative. Instead of preparing for a sustained inflationary cycle, Europe's banking sector is positioning for weakness. They view current rate hikes in the same way they viewed Jean-Claude Trichet’s hikes 15 years ago: as a near-term policy mistake that will inevitably choke off economic growth. An energy shock behaves like a tax on the consumer. As fuel and transport costs rise, households are squeezed, leaving less discretionary income for other goods and services. If businesses cannot pass these costs onto cash-strapped consumers, their margins shrink, volumes drop, and corporate credit risk surges. While the ECB looks at energy and sees inflation, commercial banks look at the same shock and see rising defaults. The Bond Buying Boom: Insiders Turn Defensive Data on Monetary Financial Institutions (MFIs) from the ECB's own database reveals a massive rush into safe assets. In May 2026, European banks loaded up on an additional 46.5 billion euros of government bonds. This follows a buying spree of 177 billion euros during the first four months of the year, bringing the total to over 220 billion euros in safe sovereign debt in just five months. Depository banks are the ultimate credit system insiders. They see deposit flows, corporate cash positions, and late payments before they show up in headline economic indicators. Piling into low-yielding government paper while the ECB is actively hiking rates is a defensive move. The banks are choosing to hold highly liquid collateral to hedge against balance sheet credit risk rather than expanding lending to fragile private borrowers. Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals , lets you do. Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it. If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you. The Euribor Futures Frown: Pricing the Policy Error The forward rate market in Europe confirms this defensive outlook. The Euribor futures curve—the Eurozone's equivalent of the term SOFR futures curve—currently exhibits a pronounced "frown." While the front end of the Euribor curve prices in the risk of one more near-term ECB rate hike, the middle and back parts of the curve bend downward. This shape tells us that: The market expects the ECB to hike rates in the short term due to hawkish rhetoric. The market does not believe this is the beginning of a structural, long-term raising cycle. Investors expect rate hikes to trigger an economic contraction, eventually forcing the ECB to pivot and cut rates. A steep, upward-sloping curve would signal a market worried about embedded, self-sustaining inflation. A frowning curve signals a market hedging against a policy error. Macro Data Confirms the Slowdown The latest macroeconomic data from the Eurozone aligns with the banks and the futures curve rather than the ECB's forecasts: German Inflation Easing : June 2026 consumer price data for Germany, the Eurozone's economic engine, unexpectedly cooled to 2.4% year-over-year. The stabilizing core index shows zero evidence of second-round wage-price spiral effects. Weak Economic Sentiment : The European Commission’s economic sentiment indexes remain weak, with only minor bounces from historical lows. Weakening Employment Expectations : Hiring plans remain depressed. Smaller and medium-sized firms are delaying investment and reducing hiring intentions to protect margins in a weak demand environment. Employment is a lagging indicator, but the stagnation in hiring shows that the feedback loop is turning. Households are becoming more cautious, spending is weakening, and credit demand is turning defensive. This is the exact environment where government bonds become the ultimate refuge, reinforcing the trend we have seen across the global credit cycle downswing and bond markets . Conclusion: A Return to the Trichet Playbook In both 2008 and 2011, the ECB famously raised interest rates during energy-driven price spikes, completely misjudging the underlying fragility of the banking and credit system. In both cases, the central bank was forced to rapidly reverse course as liquidity crises emerged. The massive bond-buying program by European banks and the inversion of the Euribor futures curve suggest that history is rhyme-matching. The banking system is quietly preparing for the economic fallout of another central bank policy mistake. This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on credit markets, European banking plumbing, and central bank policy. item.turbo:content:
ECB Policy Mistake: Why European Banks Buy Safety

ECB Policy Mistake: Why European Banks Buy Safety

European banks are doing something that looks, at first glance, completely irrational.

While mainstream commentators focus on central bank hawkishness, the European Central Bank (ECB) recently raised its policy rates and officials continue to threaten further hikes, fixated on inflation risks driven by the energy shock. Yet, the banks themselves are preparing for a very different reality.

The Inflation Illusion vs. Real Credit Risk

Normally, if you expect interest rates to go higher, you avoid government bonds to protect yourself from duration risk and mark-to-market losses. But European depository institutions are aggressively buying government debt, signaling that they do not believe the ECB's inflation narrative.

Instead of preparing for a sustained inflationary cycle, Europe's banking sector is positioning for weakness. They view current rate hikes in the same way they viewed Jean-Claude Trichet’s hikes 15 years ago: as a near-term policy mistake that will inevitably choke off economic growth.

An energy shock behaves like a tax on the consumer. As fuel and transport costs rise, households are squeezed, leaving less discretionary income for other goods and services. If businesses cannot pass these costs onto cash-strapped consumers, their margins shrink, volumes drop, and corporate credit risk surges. While the ECB looks at energy and sees inflation, commercial banks look at the same shock and see rising defaults.

The Bond Buying Boom: Insiders Turn Defensive

Data on Monetary Financial Institutions (MFIs) from the ECB's own database reveals a massive rush into safe assets.

In May 2026, European banks loaded up on an additional 46.5 billion euros of government bonds. This follows a buying spree of 177 billion euros during the first four months of the year, bringing the total to over 220 billion euros in safe sovereign debt in just five months.

Depository banks are the ultimate credit system insiders. They see deposit flows, corporate cash positions, and late payments before they show up in headline economic indicators. Piling into low-yielding government paper while the ECB is actively hiking rates is a defensive move. The banks are choosing to hold highly liquid collateral to hedge against balance sheet credit risk rather than expanding lending to fragile private borrowers.

Sponsor Spotlight: Monetary Metals

What if your gold could actually pay you every single month in more gold? That's what today's sponsor, Monetary Metals, lets you do.

Here's how it works: You still own the gold, it's still in your name, it's fully insured, but instead of just sitting there doing nothing, it actually starts working for you. Monetary Metals has built a system that turns idle gold into productive gold, earning real yield paid in physical gold, not dollars. No selling, no trading, no converting. You keep your gold and every month you get more of it.

If you're a long-term gold holder looking for a smarter way to store value, check them out at monetary-metals.com to see if they're the right fit for you.

The Euribor Futures Frown: Pricing the Policy Error

The forward rate market in Europe confirms this defensive outlook. The Euribor futures curve—the Eurozone's equivalent of the term SOFR futures curve—currently exhibits a pronounced "frown."

While the front end of the Euribor curve prices in the risk of one more near-term ECB rate hike, the middle and back parts of the curve bend downward. This shape tells us that:

  • The market expects the ECB to hike rates in the short term due to hawkish rhetoric.
  • The market does not believe this is the beginning of a structural, long-term raising cycle.
  • Investors expect rate hikes to trigger an economic contraction, eventually forcing the ECB to pivot and cut rates.

A steep, upward-sloping curve would signal a market worried about embedded, self-sustaining inflation. A frowning curve signals a market hedging against a policy error.

Macro Data Confirms the Slowdown

The latest macroeconomic data from the Eurozone aligns with the banks and the futures curve rather than the ECB's forecasts:

  • German Inflation Easing: June 2026 consumer price data for Germany, the Eurozone's economic engine, unexpectedly cooled to 2.4% year-over-year. The stabilizing core index shows zero evidence of second-round wage-price spiral effects.
  • Weak Economic Sentiment: The European Commission’s economic sentiment indexes remain weak, with only minor bounces from historical lows.
  • Weakening Employment Expectations: Hiring plans remain depressed. Smaller and medium-sized firms are delaying investment and reducing hiring intentions to protect margins in a weak demand environment.

Employment is a lagging indicator, but the stagnation in hiring shows that the feedback loop is turning. Households are becoming more cautious, spending is weakening, and credit demand is turning defensive. This is the exact environment where government bonds become the ultimate refuge, reinforcing the trend we have seen across the global credit cycle downswing and bond markets.

Conclusion: A Return to the Trichet Playbook

In both 2008 and 2011, the ECB famously raised interest rates during energy-driven price spikes, completely misjudging the underlying fragility of the banking and credit system. In both cases, the central bank was forced to rapidly reverse course as liquidity crises emerged.

The massive bond-buying program by European banks and the inversion of the Euribor futures curve suggest that history is rhyme-matching. The banking system is quietly preparing for the economic fallout of another central bank policy mistake.

This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on credit markets, European banking plumbing, and central bank policy.

item.title: Microsoft Layoffs: The AI-Driven Efficiency Excuse
item.author: khalid
item.description: Microsoft's layoffs are blamed on AI, but JOLTS and consumer surveys reveal a traditional macro freeze: a no-hiring economy in tech-hype packaging.
item.yandex:full-text: Microsoft Layoffs: The AI-Driven Efficiency Excuse Microsoft just announced another round of job cuts, laying off thousands of workers. Immediately, commentators pointed to two letters: AI. The mainstream narrative is simple: artificial intelligence is eliminating white-collar work, and the robots are taking over. While this is an emotionally compelling explanation, it completely misses the broader macroeconomic reality. AI Washing: Layoffs Under a Visionary Label When a giant tech company cuts workers at the exact moment its executives are hyping up generative AI, the conclusion seems obvious. But beneath the headlines, the story is far more complicated—and troubling. The labor market is not weakening because algorithms have suddenly become good enough to replace entire departments. Rather, companies are reacting to a traditional macroeconomic slowdown. Demand is uncertain, consumers are selective, and executives need to protect margins. To impress Wall Street, companies are wrapping cost-cutting measures in the language of innovation: "AI-driven efficiency." This practice, known as AI washing , allows businesses to package standard cyclical layoffs as forward-looking corporate evolution. Sponsor Spotlight: Monarch Juggling between different bank accounts, credit cards, and investment portfolios can make it impossible to see your true financial picture. That's where today's sponsor, Monarch , comes in. Monarch pulls all your accounts into one clean, private, and ad-free dashboard. It is a read-only, fully encrypted system that gives you total clarity over your spending, net worth, and recurring bills without selling your data. To gain calm control over your finances, learn more about Monarch today. The Real Story: JOLTS and the No-Hiring Freeze To understand what is actually happening in the labor market, we must look at turnover rather than just the headline payroll numbers. The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) reveals a very different structure: Ghost Listings : While job openings remain technically high, these are often intentions, not actions. Companies leave listings online for months without actively filling them. Hiring Freeze : Actual hiring has slowed dramatically. Companies are not expanding payrolls with any sense of urgency. Low Quit Rates : Quit rates have dropped, indicating that workers are not confident they can leave their current jobs and easily find better opportunities. This is a no-hiring economy , not a mass-firing economy. Companies are choosing not to hire rather than launching broad, explosive layoffs. For a job seeker, a hiring freeze feels identical to a recession—it doesn't matter if you aren't laid off if nobody is responding to your applications. Consumer Confidence and the "Jobs Hard to Get" Jump This freeze in hiring is showing up directly in consumer surveys. The latest report from the Conference Board reveals that the share of consumers saying "jobs are hard to get" has jumped to its highest level since early 2021. This metric is a direct reflection of real-world experience. When sending out applications leads to silence and interviews lead nowhere, consumers grow cautious. This caution dampens spending, prompting businesses to protect their cash reserves. This feedback loop is cyclical, not technological. It mirrors the dynamics observed during the recent bond market moves and credit cycle downswing , where a lack of economic momentum freezes normal activity. The "AI Regret" Wave: Bringing Humans Back If AI were truly driving these layoffs, we would see companies operating more efficiently with permanently smaller staffs. Instead, a wave of "AI regret" is setting in. A survey by OrgVue found that 40% of executives cut staff to implement AI, and 55% of them later regretted the decision. Major companies are finding that replacing human judgment is far more difficult than a slide deck suggests: Ford Motor Company : Ford recently rehired 350 veteran engineers (former employees and supplier experts) to address persistent quality issues that AI and automated systems could not resolve. Their initial quality ratings improved immediately after. Klarna : After aggressively shrinking its headcount from 3,800 to 2,000 using AI chatbots, the company had to adjust its strategy to ensure customers could always reach human support to maintain service quality. IBM : After automating routine human resources tasks, reports indicated IBM had to rehire staff for areas requiring empathy, subjectivity, and human nuance. AI is a powerful tool, but it lacks the institutional knowledge, judgment, and adaptability of experienced human workers. Conclusion: Obscuring the Macro Freeze The next time you see a headline about major tech layoffs, look past the AI-driven narrative. Microsoft, like Amazon and other tech giants, over-hired during the pandemic boom years. Now that the global economy has lost momentum, they are resetting their payrolls to align with a slower growth environment. AI is reshaping specific workflows, but the current labor market tightness is a macroeconomic freeze, not a robotic takeover. The true problem is not that humans have become obsolete; it is that opportunity has stalled in an economy that has forgotten how to expand. This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on labor markets, technology cycles, and corporate structures. item.turbo:content:
Microsoft Layoffs: The AI-Driven Efficiency Excuse

Microsoft Layoffs: The AI-Driven Efficiency Excuse

Microsoft just announced another round of job cuts, laying off thousands of workers. Immediately, commentators pointed to two letters: AI.

The mainstream narrative is simple: artificial intelligence is eliminating white-collar work, and the robots are taking over. While this is an emotionally compelling explanation, it completely misses the broader macroeconomic reality.

AI Washing: Layoffs Under a Visionary Label

When a giant tech company cuts workers at the exact moment its executives are hyping up generative AI, the conclusion seems obvious. But beneath the headlines, the story is far more complicated—and troubling.

The labor market is not weakening because algorithms have suddenly become good enough to replace entire departments. Rather, companies are reacting to a traditional macroeconomic slowdown. Demand is uncertain, consumers are selective, and executives need to protect margins.

To impress Wall Street, companies are wrapping cost-cutting measures in the language of innovation: "AI-driven efficiency." This practice, known as AI washing, allows businesses to package standard cyclical layoffs as forward-looking corporate evolution.

Sponsor Spotlight: Monarch

Juggling between different bank accounts, credit cards, and investment portfolios can make it impossible to see your true financial picture. That's where today's sponsor, Monarch, comes in.

Monarch pulls all your accounts into one clean, private, and ad-free dashboard. It is a read-only, fully encrypted system that gives you total clarity over your spending, net worth, and recurring bills without selling your data.

To gain calm control over your finances, learn more about Monarch today.

The Real Story: JOLTS and the No-Hiring Freeze

To understand what is actually happening in the labor market, we must look at turnover rather than just the headline payroll numbers. The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) reveals a very different structure:

  • Ghost Listings: While job openings remain technically high, these are often intentions, not actions. Companies leave listings online for months without actively filling them.
  • Hiring Freeze: Actual hiring has slowed dramatically. Companies are not expanding payrolls with any sense of urgency.
  • Low Quit Rates: Quit rates have dropped, indicating that workers are not confident they can leave their current jobs and easily find better opportunities.

This is a no-hiring economy, not a mass-firing economy. Companies are choosing not to hire rather than launching broad, explosive layoffs. For a job seeker, a hiring freeze feels identical to a recession—it doesn't matter if you aren't laid off if nobody is responding to your applications.

Consumer Confidence and the "Jobs Hard to Get" Jump

This freeze in hiring is showing up directly in consumer surveys. The latest report from the Conference Board reveals that the share of consumers saying "jobs are hard to get" has jumped to its highest level since early 2021.

This metric is a direct reflection of real-world experience. When sending out applications leads to silence and interviews lead nowhere, consumers grow cautious. This caution dampens spending, prompting businesses to protect their cash reserves.

This feedback loop is cyclical, not technological. It mirrors the dynamics observed during the recent bond market moves and credit cycle downswing, where a lack of economic momentum freezes normal activity.

The "AI Regret" Wave: Bringing Humans Back

If AI were truly driving these layoffs, we would see companies operating more efficiently with permanently smaller staffs. Instead, a wave of "AI regret" is setting in. A survey by OrgVue found that 40% of executives cut staff to implement AI, and 55% of them later regretted the decision.

Major companies are finding that replacing human judgment is far more difficult than a slide deck suggests:

  • Ford Motor Company: Ford recently rehired 350 veteran engineers (former employees and supplier experts) to address persistent quality issues that AI and automated systems could not resolve. Their initial quality ratings improved immediately after.
  • Klarna: After aggressively shrinking its headcount from 3,800 to 2,000 using AI chatbots, the company had to adjust its strategy to ensure customers could always reach human support to maintain service quality.
  • IBM: After automating routine human resources tasks, reports indicated IBM had to rehire staff for areas requiring empathy, subjectivity, and human nuance.

AI is a powerful tool, but it lacks the institutional knowledge, judgment, and adaptability of experienced human workers.

Conclusion: Obscuring the Macro Freeze

The next time you see a headline about major tech layoffs, look past the AI-driven narrative.

Microsoft, like Amazon and other tech giants, over-hired during the pandemic boom years. Now that the global economy has lost momentum, they are resetting their payrolls to align with a slower growth environment.

AI is reshaping specific workflows, but the current labor market tightness is a macroeconomic freeze, not a robotic takeover. The true problem is not that humans have become obsolete; it is that opportunity has stalled in an economy that has forgotten how to expand.

This analysis is part of our Global Macro series, focusing on labor markets, technology cycles, and corporate structures.
